sections.title: Chairman's Chat
sections.content:
Martin Baxter reports

Wow – what a fantastic Spring for the XC hounds; although I suspect that the rowdy conditions didn’t suit everybody.  On a personal note, I have been battling demons: a family bereavement, a twisted ankle (walking down from Dodd Fell in trainers), and a paragliding scare…

For those of you that didn’t read my flight log on the forum, towards the end of April I had a big collapse, which developed into a cascade, at Windbank.  My tracklog recorded a maximum downwards velocity of 17m/s and I only recovered 500’ above the deck.  I don’t mind admitting that it’s rather unsettled me.

It was pretty windy and I led the charge to walk down the hill to launch.  It had been really buoyant for most of the flight but conditions changed towards the end.  I struggled to gain enough height to bag the final turn point on the NCS task and allowed my competitive spirit to get the better of common sense.   At the time I thought that I might have spun the glider whilst trying to get back into a bullet of a thermal that I’d taken over the back low, but with hindsight I think that I flew into the rotor.

I don’t remember much, just suddenly falling out of the sky.  I immediately thought about pulling my reserve: but didn’t.  I suppose that I was hoping that my wing would sort itself out, which it eventually did.  Initially I didn’t look up at my wing, only noticing it when we were both pointing vertically at the ground.  Did I pull my feet in: probably not.  Did I brake the dive: I like to think so.  Either way my wing opened with a thwack.  I countered the yaw, crabbed out of more potential rotor and landed safely.  Phew!  It only seemed like a few seconds to me, but 2 witnesses said that the cascade lasted about 10 seconds and looked ‘terrifying’.

I’ve re-lived the incident many times in my head, and lost quite a lot of sleep worrying about it.  Should I do an SIV course?  Should I step back to an EN B wing?  Should I give up altogether?

I’ve always been a bit sceptical of SIV courses giving false confidence – conducted in smooth air with full anticipation of what’s coming.  Nevertheless, they come highly recommended by experienced pilots and, after my scare I was willing to consider anything.  I did some research and came up with a shortlist of 4 providers.  They all take place abroad and none of them come cheap.  In addition to all the costs you would normally expect to pay for an overseas paragliding trip, you can expect to pay at least another £1,000 for the instruction.  But what price safety/your life?  Crammed into the time available they are all very intensive and I wonder how much skill is actually retained.  The general consensus is that they should be repeated frequently to maintain muscle memory.  The courses seem to take place in Spring and Autumn.  I think that if I were going to do one then I’d want to do it in Spring – at the start of the UK flying season.

Should I step back to an EN B?  My last wing was an EN B, but I’ve owned 2 EN C wings previously and never had any issues with them.  I bought the Flow Fusion more out of convenience when my old wing died, but I do enjoy the increased performance (although I’m struggling to master the basic skill of a straight-line big ears on it).  The question arises as to what sort of flying I want to do.   Whilst I respect and admire the dedication, pre-planning and competitiveness of the XC hounds; the thrill of landing by my car, having a pint, and being home in time for dinner tends to outweigh that of landing in the middle of nowhere and the ‘adventure’ of getting back.  Unlike hardy XC pilots, after a long day out, there is no way I could contemplate doing the same the following day.  Recently I’ve been attempting the NCS challenges but, since they often consist of into wind legs, a higher performance wing is arguably more useful for that than it is for a ‘downwind dash’.

Should I give up altogether?  I suppose that it’s something that we’ll all have to contemplate one day, probably due to age, injury or illness.  Better to bow out when you’re on top of your game rather than slowly drifting into a lack of currency whilst denying the inevitable?  Flying requires commitment, but rather than the pressure to get out at every opportunity I’m beginning to wonder if there is a middle ground where I can just fly when it’s convenient.  I admit that recently, having abstained, it’s been a relief not to have to study the weather and worry about making the wrong decision – wasted journey or missed opportunity.

I have flown once since my scare.  Not the best of conditions for ‘getting back into the saddle’ – a light wind, thermic day at Stags Fell.  I managed to climb to base but every twitch of the wing made me feel very nervous.  I’m hoping that time, and in particular airtime, will allow me to regain my confidence.

We all know that the consequences of making a mistake whilst flying can be catastrophic.  That’s why we are so nervous when we learn: it’s just not natural.  But every time we fly and survive without incident our confidence builds, perhaps until we become over confident, pushing the dangers to the back of our minds.  My incident has been a useful reminder of how vulnerable I am.  Is it really any different to riding a motorbike or bicycle, driving a car, or being a pedestrian?

sections.title: Coaching
sections.content:
Pete Logan, Chief Coach reports

Congratulations to James Maxwell, a new member last year but obviously putting the hours in. He passed his pilot rating last month. .. And then celebrated with a massive 97km XC!

I appreciate that this is late advice, but this spring has been a bit of a breakthrough for me on keeping warm. Warm (or at least not cold) pilots are happy pilots and happy pilots are paying attention and enjoying their flying. I seem to be on the distribution of people that suffer more from the cold, especially hands and toes, hence my efforts to get this problem sorted out.

There are a couple of incidents I often remember that bring this into focus. The year I came back to flying I had an autumn trip to Chamonix. We had a good XC day planned and I took off from the Brevent and headed south. Within 30 minutes I had two painful blocks of ice for hands and was not actively piloting. I had a few biggish asymmetrics and was not enjoying this, knowing the pain in my hands was not going to get better. I peeled off from the ridge to calmer air and landed back at Chamonix, missing most of a big XC day.

Another time, I’m 70km into a spring flight from Model Ridge. The batteries die on my gloves and the cold and pain seeps in. It’s a big day and I’m desperate to stay up and get a long flight in but I’m also separated from the gaggle from miles back and too close to the spreadout from the Peaks which has shut out the sun. I’m grimly hanging on and each time I get to base and go on glide, I’m shouting out obscenities because, apparently, that helps with pain…. https://www.keele.ac.uk/research/researchnews/2020/june/traditional-swearing/tolerate-pain.php 

Now to how I’ve solved the problem. First of all is keeping a warm core. If your body and head are cold then you have no chance with your extremities.


From bottom to top:

Merino wool long sleeve top (blue). It’s great in hot or cold, stands up to mistreatment and doesn’t smell. Believe the Merino hype.

Long armed thin fleece (red). Zip up and offset neck i.e. the zip opening doesn’t line up with the rest of the openings which helps keep the cold air out. Arms are long enough to cover my wrists.

Running top (black). Dense weave and mostly windproof. For the colder days. This is the first layer I discard when it warms up.

Softshell fleece/jacket (dark grey). Indestructible Rab Polartec top with long arms and thumb holes in the cuffs.

Thin synthetic insulation jacket (navy). Shower and wind proof. I used to start spring in a massive 650 fill down jacket but I think that let in too much breeze and the feathers have slowly leaked out of the arms so these last two layers are less bulky and seem warmer.
Shower proof anorak (white). Reflects the hot sun beating down on launch. I didn’t mind modifying it to house my radio and whip antenna. It’s been useful just having it when there’s been rain on landing out etc.  

Notice that there’s six layers on the arms here. I don’t go for T shirts or gilets as it wastes a chance to get another air trapping layer on your arms. I wear some kind of walking trousers and usually that’s fine as my upper legs don’t feel the cold and I have the pod too. In winter I’ll put on long johns on TO. I’ve spared you a photo of that. On TO I remove my boots and put on the knee length wool socks so I’m double socked. I just have to remember to wiggle my toes every so often and they’re then fine.

For the head, I have the thick, fluffy buff around the neck which the missus bought me. It’s been a huge improvement over a regular buff. It will come up above my nose when I need it to and can be quite toasty. (https://www.ebay.co.uk/itm/135533519555)  I switched my lid recently from a Plusmax where I also needed to wear a wooly beanie underneath to a Bolle Backline Visor. It has ear covers, great insulation and the visor pulls down and has a seal so that it rarely mists.

Now the tricky bit:

All the above is just preparation for keeping my hands warm.

Thin satin like undergloves (black at the top). Don’t impede dexterity and every little layer helps.
Gin Heated gloves. The original batteries were pretty rubbish and I didn’t fancy buying the same replacements at an exorbitant price so I made my own. Good quality LG 3500mAh 18650 batteries, a BMS and the right barrel jack are cheaper, more powerful and last longer. https://www.nubattery.co.uk/lg-mj1-18650-battery These batteries will do 7 hours with the heat on medium. It doesn’t get warm but it does stop the real cold getting in.
Brake muffs do not go on the brakes! They’re a faff and a safety hazard using them as intended. They go in my cockpit and after the first climb I take them out and pull them over my forearms for an extra layer.
Tucked inside my muffs are a thin, waterproof motorcycle mitts that I’ve modified to being a trigger mitt by cutting an index finger loose and sewing up the resulting hole with an insert. I just couldn’t find what I wanted to buy, hence I had to do my own sewing. These go on as a last resort but are safely tucked down my brake muffs when I don’t need them.

Lastly, I keep the blood moving. On glide I lower my arms, occasionally rummage in the cockpit for a snack, windmill my arms from the shoulders too. The end result of all this was a seven hour flight for 200km last week (more to come on this) and whilst I had cold hands at base, it was not painful. A few days later I still have a little tingling in my index fingers. That’s partly brought on by cold but mostly the braking.  

What could I do in the future?
Mylar layers like they have in space suits would be great but reduced breathability and condensing sweat are going to be a nightmare on take off and walking back up. I like the idea of reflecting body heat back in though.
Next year, I’ll try a stretchy base layer top and leggings and see if we can improve further. (FDX Sports)
Rob U recommended wrist gaiters, so I’m going to throw on a pair of those next year. https://www.newforestclothing.co.uk/products/extremities-powerliner-wrist-gaiter

sections.title: Carlsberg Day
sections.content:
by Pete D, flying with Chris K on 10th May

“I shouldn’t have gone in that one” came over the radio as I dribbled over the back of Cautley into the centre of the Howgills with only 1100 metres.  My sentiments entirely, but Chris was even lower!  The wind was brisk, but not unmanageable, and this plus a few sustainers helped avoid getting stuck in one of the gills.  We escaped to Orton Scar, but only the “rubbish bit”, where Chris was low enough to be looking for Roman coinage.  I flew over to offer some moral support.

Perhaps it was because we had already spent an hour getting battered in Cautley bowl that leaving several hundred meters below cloud base seemed a good idea?  Quite why it was so turbulent I have yet to establish: possibly the wind direction was too southerly, the wind speed too great, or the wind gradient too steep.  Either way, the dramatic setting for England’s highest above-ground spout felt more like a washing machine, and for a while I was wondering if the usual crew were faring any better on Parlick.  However, wanting to avoid the crowds and stress after another late night, and the desire to fly in the Dales, had steered us away from red rose country.

By some miracle, Chris managed to latch onto the very base of my climb, and after half an hour of patience we were at 1900 metres over the fertile Vale of Eden. Downstream led to Centre Parcs, Penrith, Carlisle and previous cross-border forays to Langholm, Lockerbie and Dumfries.  But not today.  The abundance of altitude and the blue sky further ahead, compared with pretty cumulus over the Pennines, let me be persuaded that heading to the desolate landscape of the Cross Fell range was a better plan.  Certainly not the fastest, nor possibly the most reliable of options, thirty minutes later I came very close to regretting the decision as the tables were turned.  Chris escaped up the shallow slope of Little Dun Fell and popped off the top.  I missed the opportunity and had a go at scaring the fell ponies as well of all manner of smaller mammals, birds and probably invertebrates, and needless to say myself, by hanging on to the upper slopes of Cross Fell.  It felt like the spirits wanted me down; Fiends Fell was not going to give up its quarry easily.  An angry one from the Middle Tongue gulley got me above ridge height, but I had to head back into the maelstrom for a second go before being released.

At 2400 metres over the Pennines everything looks possible, especially with clouds ahead, a good drift and Spadeadam closed for the weekend.  The Solway 50 kilometres to our west and Tynemouth 60 to our east; this was unchartered territory.  However, 1000 metres over Wark Forest, with no roads for kilometres in any direction, quickly starts to feel uncomfortable.  Some lumpy terrain in the Spadeadam Wastes, a few beeps, and thirty minutes later we were back in orbit admiring the expanse of Kielder reservoir.  The novelty of having to fly west to round Otterburn and then we were exiting the Pennines and flying alongside the Cheviots.

Another cross-border raid, but this time with two grand in the bank, and achieved via the central spine.  A wait for Chris’ climb to strengthen south of Jedburgh allowed plenty of time to take in the view of the Scottish Borders and plan the next phase; we had a goal at Galashiels but who cares, or there were a pair of random breast-like hills (paps?) near Melrose, or head towards Kelso following the Tweed valley and the now-obvious approaching sea-breeze from the east.

The meteo wind had turned south westerly as forecast sealing the decision, and at 18:00 and 2200 metres just north of Kelso we could see that if the sea-breeze front held together there was a chance of the coast a mere 35 kilometres away.  The thought remained unspoken between us.  We headed northeast on a very buoyant line under the decaying clouds, picking up weak climbs along the way.  A last climb to 1900 metres just south of Duns at 18:25 and it was 18 kilometres to the coast with a 20kmph tailwind.  

The coast in the bag, relax in euphoria at the unplanned epic.  I was ignorantly snapping away, when Chris, ever so slightly lower than me, spotted the windmills ahead were spinning vigorously in a southerly?  Approaching Coldingham at 600 metres I put in a turn and read 80kmph ground speed heading north. Bugger, the day had another twist up its sleeve.  Suddenly landing on the cliff became a really daft idea; getting further from the sea seemed far more sensible.  Crabbing backwards on full bar in front of Coldingham towards some big fields and then down in choppy air, but ultimately vertically, and with a gentle touchdown in the field next to Chris.  Phew! 

A fairytale day with extraordinary conditions, lows and highs, new and amazing scenery, a coast landing at 18:50, in Scotland, from the Dales, 100 miles away, a personal best (just) and pretty much all with company.  Uber refused a ride from Coldingham to Berwick, but very fortuitously the only taxi driver in the village was only two minutes away.  We then stepped onto the delayed east coast mainline train meaning we could get back to Leeds that evening, calling off the emergency accommodation offer with Rosie's brother in Durham, phoned my mother to wish her Happy Birthday, met Richards Meek and Carter in York returning from their Parlick goal of Dunbar, and got the last train back to Ilkley along with loads of people in inappropriate "outfits" who had clearly had too much lager.

I had already decided to can Sunday as it really could not get any better, and I desperately needed some rest!  Later in the day I cycled out to Cautley to pick up the bag-o-spanners car under another amazing sky for once not even regretting my choice not to fly.

I don’t even like Carlsberg.

sections.title: First XC Flight
sections.content:
By James Maxwell on 23rd May

Setting up on Gragareth. Photo by Chris Kay

Since passing my CP rating in December last year, it's safe to say I've become a little bit obsessed with paragliding. With the lure of XC becoming ever harder to resist, I knew it was time to get my pilot rating. I sat the exam and waited (im)patiently for the results, which came just in time for the last of the amazing weather in May.

With a 5 star forecast and a strong Westerly wind, plans started to be hatched. We met at Cowen Bridge to discuss options, and after some debate between Gragareth and Whernside, Gragareth was selected for convenience as much as anything else. Following the short walk to the take off, we began setting up and were soon joined by more pilots. It was at this point that the nerves started to kick in. The launch site had become quite crowded and the worry of being dragged over the sea of gliders was at the forefront of my mind. I'd seen this happen on multiple occasions in comparable wind strength so I chose to lay my glider out across the wind. Having spent much of the winter binge watching Greg Hamerton's YouTube videos on repeat, I snaked the glider up in a (surprisingly successful) cobra launch. I then side stepped away from the busy runway and got airborne.

The launch site was fairly shallow and with the wind direction on the day not producing a great deal of ridge lift. Thankfully, the site offers a consistent "house thermal" that triggers from a nearby farmhouse. I scurried along the ridge until I felt the tail end of the thermal drawing up the slope, then turned into wind to locate the core. After a few circles I was up to around 900m where I unfortunately dropped out. Watching the rest of the gaggle, I managed to just about get re-established and make the (perhaps premature) jump over to the Whernside.

During the crossing, I unfortunately lost all my altitude and ended up back below the ridge. Thankfully there was enough wind on Whernside to soar, and following Chris Kay, I jumped into a 3m/s climb straight to base. At this point far over the back of Whernside, there were limited opportunities to find another soarable ridge and I was conscious that I couldn't afford to sink out again. Thankfully, the strong wind had produced a series of parallel cloud streets that made the route choice particularly simple.

Following the high ground under the street, the flight went smoothly for around another 30km until an ill-timed jump to a dwindling cloud put me in unpleasant sink. With the sink alarm blaring, I pushed half-bar under a darkening cloud and just as I began to think my flight might be over, my wing rocked back and crushed me into my seat. I cranked it round and rode a 6m/s climb straight back to base in only 4 turns!

After this point the air mass seemed to produce less sink and I pushed on east following the gps markers for the gaggle that had by now shot away towards their distant goal. Much to my surprise, I soon arrived over Harrogate and shortly after, York. With a significant PB in the bag and minimal sensation left in my semi frozen legs, I noticed a large unbuilt line of fields heading in towards the train station. I followed this in and spiralled down to get some blood flow back in the landing gear. After touchdown I checked my phone to find I had flown a distance of 98km. With 100km so tantalisingly close, I'm now more desperate than ever to get out again!

sections.title: Mince for the Low Save
sections.content:
Pete Logan reports

The three photos above are of Ovenden Moor, west of Halifax and 18 km to the south of me taking the dog a walk on Baildon Moor. It’s worth pausing to take a look at what’s happening. The fire was put out the same day so thankfully there wasn’t too much damage done.

We’re looking at the 110m tall turbines, spread over 1 km east to west. That means there’s ~160 m spacing between them. The fire starts ~300 m in front of the first turbine and there’s a fairly stiff easterly breeze. It’s inverted but not so much that people didn’t go XC that day.

I don’t think the fire is contributing to much of the bubbling we’re seeing, it’s small on the scale of moorland fires. I think it’s illustrating what’s happening with the wind and sun on the moor. The graph below is of the amount of solar heat and light delivered to every square metre of the moor - let’s say about 500W ish / m2. The aerial photo of the fire at the bottom is about 400m by 150m. That’s 30 Megawatts! The flame front is ~300 m by ~1 m and is generating 5 kW / m2, or 1.5 Megawatts in total. Fires are great for showing us direction and might be triggers but unless they’re massive, they’re not the source of the thermal.


The point I’m trying to get across is that the smoke is illustrating the thermal behaviour more than what the fire is causing by itself. Now that we’ve established that, what more can we say?

Well, the air is pretty close to the ground for about the first 500m before a bubble generally pops up above 200 m height. It’s the best part of a kilometre before there’s a release that gets up past 500 m. It’s wanting to rise, but it hasn’t got enough of an excuse to release early on. The map of the moor shows that just after the turbines the moor peaks at 445m  and then descends to a reservoir. This is the most likely cause for the air finally releasing.

BTW - Fill Belly Flat - what a top name.

Now to the point of the article. How low can that low save be and what do you need to do to make it work?

Your chances of hitting the column that is rising directly out of the moor is going to be something like one in ten. It’s much more likely that you’ll fly down into the stuff that is bubbling but still attached to the ground. You’ll be able to stay up, although it will be low and variable / turbulent. Be on your guard, active piloting is going to be needed. There air will be telling you a lot so you need to be able to turn flat and efficiently, straighten up into better lift, throw in quick reversals when you hit bad sink. Always be recentering, the goal here is to find a way to have the vario positive all the way around the turn and then build on that. Do this whilst you’re building a mental map of where and how the air is going to release… and keep an eye on what the birds are doing. What are the other signs on the ground that could be helping you get a handle on staying up? If it all stops lifting you’ll need to decide quickly on re-acquiring upwind or downwind.

I’m beginning to regret using the word “mincing” in the title. The above seems way more active than mincing would suggest. There are times though where you know you’re in a bubble of air that’s rising a little and circulating but that has not yet released. In one case I remember, I knew it had to as it approached the steeper side of a shallow escarpment slope so I hung on with gentle, flat turns for 8 km & 20 minutes (orange and yellow section) just searching for the most lifty sections.

Becoming skilled at thisis  going to take a long time if you’re always doing this close to the ground and seconds away from landing. You can practice when you’re high as to whether you can feel the glider drawing you into rising air. Searching for better lift upwind and downwind of where you’re currently climbing too is going to help you get a feel for the shape of lift you’re in.

And if you do find yourself drawn to find lift near a fire plume? In my experience it generally tends to be better upwind of smoke. In my experience it seems like they trigger air in front of them quite well, giving me the best lift before I actually get to the plume. It’s complex, the length of fire burning and other factors could be coming into play. The one place I did see pilots getting lift was from a massive brush fire burning and rapidly spreading upslope at the Brits in Pedro Bernardo, Spain some years ago. I was near it but didn’t go in it. Other pilots were getting a right kicking inside it. Thankfully we rarely get fires of that size in the UK.

sections.title: Danger Areas: GVS, HIRTA, Laser & Small Arms Ranges
sections.content:
Rob Urselmann reports

We can all imagine what a high power radio/microwave transmission (HIRTA) might do to the bag of meat that is you the pilot. If you wonder what a power station exhaust or faster-than-speed-of-sound gas vent (GVS) might do to a plane, let be our flimsy wings, GVS - the little DANGER AREA that could ruin your day! is a must watch. The third one, and one I hadn’t even considered until I was doing this, are high-power (research & military) lasers.  And then, there’s the off-chance you might find yourself unlucky enough to bomb out on a small arms range with the punters shooting away.

Looking at some different apps with airspace overlays online a few weeks ago, I realised that my XCTRACK app wasn’t showing any of them!

Example: the GVS Nether Kellet SFC-850m (National Gas compression station) near Lancaster. If you’ve flown there (I haven’t) I’m certain you’ll have noticed the massive quarry, but did you consciously mark the GVS ?

Following a conversation with pilots using various apps and the Telegram XCTrack user group, we surmise the following;

Whilst all apps ultimately (seem to) get their data from asselect.uk (which gets them from the NATS Aeronautical Information Service ENR 5.3), there is a HIRTA/GVS toggle that is optional for the app automatic download.

-  FlySkyHigh includes HIRTA/GVS, but not small arms

-  XCGuide requires USER ACTION to ensure file generation from asselect.uk has HIRTA/GVS enabled (not default), and only shows when also "Q" airspace is enabled in the app. Doesn’t show small arms.

-  XCTrack (via airspace.xcontest.org) does NOT include any. UPDATE 23th May: As a result of this discussion, GVS are now included in the auto-updates for XCTrack, but HIRTAs (and Laser) are still not.

So, for my own XCTRACK use I manually downloaded the asselect.uk airspace file including HIRTA/GVS/LASER and then stripped out everything else.

For XCTrack , this can be loaded & shown in addition to the normal (automatic) xcontest airspace updates:

  1. Upload the file to your instrument/phone running XCTrack
  2. selecting the file under >Preferences >Airspaces and obstacles > FILES > import openair files (the grey bar on top)
  3. tick the box next to the file name in the list

For other apps I’m afraid you’ll need to find a friendly knowledgeable user of that specific app (not me).


From asselect.uk, you can load HIRTA/GVS (& laser) as Restricted (R) or Warning/Danger (Q) airspace.

Difference between loading them as Q or R space:

- 'R'(estricted, shown in red), like the usual No-Gos, with all the distance warnings etc

- 'Q' (Danger, shown in brown), does NOT show distances and warnings in the airspace widget so you rely purely on you seeing them on your map widget.

User choice to pick which you prefer.

Whilst on the topic, I ended up also extracting the Small Arms Ranges directly from the AIP ENR 5.3 and created them as Q airspace.

For those that want to use them, I have put the files on Google Drive for sharing {UPDATED, Dropbox no longer works without signing up :-(  )

https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1eqgrlt1BIUrN5rAl1a5rTIgEhuz_2gLQ?usp=sharing 

  • HIRTA/Laser as Q or  R space (no GVS, use this for XCTrack until HIRTA’s added)
  • GVS/HIRTA/Laser as Q space  (in case you use an app that doesn’t include GVS either)
  • Small Arms Ranges as Q space

And whilst we’re at the topic of manual airspace files, if you haven’t got it already, for completeness sake there’s also a copy of all UK railway stations as “bow-tie” W airspace (show green and don’t generate any alarms), created by Lewis John. Rather than tiny waypoint dots, these are nicely visible whilst flying and you’re wondering where to land after that ginormous unplanned XC (ey James ).

Any questions or comments, please feel free to reach out.

sections.title: Dragon Hike & Fly
sections.content:
Paul Rowntree reports

With a week until the start, the forecast was looking okay, the diary was clear, I just needed to get my head around a 4.5hr drive there and taking part in a 2-day event with no assistance.  Hitching was my solution to the latter, so I bagged one of the last slots.

The course around the Black Mountains and briefing was issued via YouTube on Friday afternoon.  I landed at the campsite near Crickhowell at dusk.  Eating my porridge watching the sun light up the first two turnpoints, game on, let’s see how I get on.  The chat was this is doable in a day.  With the easterly forecast being potentially too strong, the starting line at 9am from the campsite was slightly reduced with 61 pilots lined up.

The campsite waking up with TP1 on the far right and the final TP in the centre, after a loop around the mountains.

Walking up, we saw the first pilots, who ran out of the campsite, ridge soar the early gentle winds towards TP2, we’ll definitely get a flight in.  By the time I was soaring along, thermals were starting and the wind strengthening.  The more skilled dodged having to walk up to TP 2 and experience the challenges of launching in last year’s dead bracken in a now strongish wind, but with the next section of the course heading east towards the Pandy Ridge I don’t think anyone was able to fly to the next TP on the other side of the valley, even the pilots who managed to climb to the c6,000ft base.

The field started to string out.  With world ranking points being given, it attracted three Swiss pilots, Noe Court being one, who seems to be a professional paraglider and is currently ranked No 1 in the CIVL H&F rankings.  He traversed the ridges with his friend Jean Morisetti into the wind finding lift of varying sources to avoid too much leg work.  They then flew the length of the Pandy Ridge to the compulsory landing field.  Unlike most of the field, they backed their flying to walk back up Pandy to fly across the 5km gap to Skirrid.  Noe found a thermal behind Pandy to take him to base, bag Skirrid, then fly c20km downwind into goal in 6.5hrs.  Jean didn’t make it but had enough breathing space from Tom Webb and Keith “Bud” Paterson to claim second.  Tom unfortunately couldn’t make the flight all the way back, so had another mountain to climb, whereas Bud’s thermal from Skirrid took him to base for a glide into goal to claim third an hour behind Noe and 15 mins after Jean.  Jacob Aubery was about to take fourth, but his final flight didn’t quite make ESS.  Tom, now back in the air, made ESS 9hrs after the start with Jacob 1 min behind.

Noe Court at the compulsory landing field at the foot of Pandy

With a 12 hr first day window, you might expect quite a few to complete the course in the day, but no, Aaron Hall was the only other pilot to complete that challenge.  Essentially as the day grew, so did the wind, there were few flights traversing the Black mountains.  When I arrived at Pandy ridge 9.5hrs in, there was a lot of south in the wind so I flew three sections of it, top landing, poseying up and walking around sections.  Those that arrived an hour later, found it had become quite buoyant and flew the entire length!

Two pilots arriving at Skirrid for an evening flight having walked from Pandy in the distance.

I arrived at Skirrid at 8:30, which was the flying cut-off time, a frustrating walk down for me.  Of note, it took Bud 25mins to complete the course once he took off from here!  Those that were camping/with support finished there for the night to start Sunday with a flight.  Everyone had to stop by 9pm.  I hitched a lift to the other side of Aberystwyth, where I was left at a pub.  What I didn’t think through in my plans was that it is now dark, hitching probably won’t work, so it was a taxi back for me with a refreshment whilst I waited, however looking back at the tracklogs, three zoomed past me, I should have given it a go.  I was lucky to get a lift from just one other person that seemed to be moving in the morning, but I was 20mins late for the 7am re-start.

A Sunday morning traffic jam covering the miles to Magic

Flying started at 7:30, half a dozen glided over me!  Despite a 30min costing navigational error (I didn’t realise there was a footpath crossing the railway line, you need to zoom in on some digital maps to see all the paths) I met up with most of those that flew off Skirrid at some stage on our walk to Magic.  I managed to beat the high winds to take off and enjoy a rapid leg saving flight to ESS to claim 13th, 16.5hrs after the start.  23/61 pilots finished before the 4pm cut off-time.

The first to catch a thermal, if only I made that, I would have climbed one less hill!!

As ever at these events, there is a great group of competitors and being a race to goal, we’re all on similar routes so you end up having some good long conversations covering the miles on foot.  Despite the wind, I managed 10 flights covering c19km of the 55km course, as the crow flies.  These events are won in the air, but I also discovered that unpacking, packing, then dealing with the poor packing in high winds from the previous time eats up a lot more time than normal.

Some of the first in the air

The Malvern team used a campsite about 2/3rds of the way around the course, which was a more logical thing to do.  The “do you wait to try and catch a thermal to avoid walking up the next hill or just get on with it” conundrum will always exist.

A point of note from the weekend is that I had my first major collapse, which I was pleased that after watching Jocky Sanderson’s recent recovery videos for a second time led to me making the correct input quickly, fly straight, and my EN-A glider snapped back into shape instantly meaning I lost less than 10m in height.  It was my own fault it happened, there were some very strong thermals only a few metres wide and I thought if I circled enough, they would develop into a wider thermal, I was fairly high at the time with a safety margin.  In hindsight, flying banked into one of these bullet thermals is going to knock the air out of your wing, ones to leave alone in the future!

This is a link to the tracklogs - https://lt.flymaster.net/?pev=1&grp=6677&d=800177875#.  Capturing the atmosphere of the weekend very well is Ben Bowler’s 30min video - https://youtu.be/qSNHlH2j0c0.  For anyone tempted to give it a go, you need a pilot qualification now, the X-Dales (Sept) and 1-day X-Lakes (June) are great for first timers and regulars alike.

sections.title: Safety
sections.content:
Clive Sury reports

Is a Climbing Helmet Safe for Paragliding?

Each year I come across paragliders choosing to use climbing helmets and therefore, I’ve pulled together the article below to help people make an informed choice regarding their headgear safety.

At the very top of this article (in case anyone doesn’t make it through reading it) it’s essential that readers know that it’s a BHPA rule that you wear an approved helmet. Non-compliance and your insurance may not be valid!

https://www.bhpa.co.uk/documents/index.php?doc=BHPA_Rules.pdf

 

Comparing Climbing Helmet EN12492 vs Paragliding Helmet EN966 Safety Standards

If you’re an outdoor enthusiast who both climbs and flies, you might wonder: Can I use my climbing helmet for paragliding?

It’s a fair question. After all, helmets look similar, and they’re all built to protect your head—right? Not quite. The truth lies in the safety standards that govern helmet design and testing. Let’s take a closer look at the two relevant standards: EN12492 for climbing helmets, and EN966 for paragliding helmets.

🧗 EN12492: Climbing Helmet Safety Standard

EN12492 is the European standard for mountaineering helmets. It focuses on:
- ✅ Protection from vertical impacts like falling rocks
- ✅ Some side and rear protection
- ✅ Secure fit to stay on during falls
- ✅ Lightweight and breathable for high exertion activities

Climbing helmets are optimized for rock impact, tumbling falls, and all-day wear on the mountain. They’re not tested for the types of high-speed, multidirectional impacts that can occur in flight.

🪂 EN966: Paragliding and Hang Gliding Helmet Standard

EN966 is the safety standard specifically for air sports helmets, including paragliding. These helmets must offer:
- ✅ High-speed impact protection
- ✅ Extended coverage around the skull and base of the head
- ✅ Aerodynamic shape to reduce drag and neck strain
- ✅ Resistance to sharp object penetration
- ✅ Secure retention under rotational and glancing impacts

These helmets are designed for crashes during takeoff, landing, or mid-flight incidents—scenarios that climbing helmets are simply not built to handle.

🛡️ Side-by-Side Comparison

Feature

EN12492 (Climbing)

EN966 (Paragliding)

Impact protection

Vertical + moderate side

Multi-angle, high-energy

Penetration resistance

Moderate

High

Aerodynamic design

Not required

Required

Coverage

Limited

Extended (sides + back)

Retention system

Fall resistance

Air-speed crash tested

Ventilation

High

Moderate (balanced with safety)

❌ Can You Use a Climbing Helmet for Paragliding?

Short answer: No.

While a climbing helmet might seem like a convenient option, it doesn’t meet the safety criteria for paragliding. It lacks the aerodynamic design, coverage, and impact resistance needed in flight. Using it puts you at real risk in the event of a crash.

✅ What Should You Use Instead?

Always choose an EN966-certified helmet for paragliding. These are built to withstand the specific demands of flying—whether you're thermalling high above the mountains or landing in a tight field.

Some helmets may be dual-certified (e.g., EN966 + EN1077 for snow sports), offering versatility without compromising safety. But make sure EN966 is clearly listed on the certification label.

🎯 Final Thoughts

Helmets are not “one-size-fits-all.”
Climbing helmets protect against falling rocks and bumps on rock faces. Paragliding helmets protect you at speed, in open air, and during potentially violent landings.

If you’re flying, trust the gear made for flying.
Choose an EN966 helmet—and fly safe.

P.S.  In case the motivation here is weight, I had a quick look on the Sick and Wrong website which shows a Supair Pilot helmet available that is just 380g which is only marginally more than a lightweight climbing helmet at 225g I found online.  Only marginally heavier for an EN966 helmet.

Safe flying,

Clive.

Pete Logan reports

The Niviuk Arrow harness has been pretty popular in the last couple of years, mainly due to its killer feature of being able to put a full packed lunch and squeezy carton of juice in the easy access cockpit. It  did come with a comically large reserve pouch though, which causes your reserve to wander about inside it. Niviuk have issued a tailored filler to keep the reserve in place. Available from your dealer now. Steve Giles at The Sick and the Wrong has them in if you bought it there and will send them out.

sections.title: From the Archive
sections.content: Anybody know this young looking chap?

sections.title: Competition Scene (Spring 2025)
sections.content:
Ed Cleasby  (Comps)

Less than a week ago I packed in a parched field, chatting with a friendly farmer. We both bemoaned the lack of rain, admittedly he had greater cause than me to complain. Within days he had his wish as one of the driest Spring’s came to an end with a splash … an extended wet payback seems to have set in.  With reasonable justification (44 seasons worth), I believe it was the best UK wide, season opener I can recall.  Not simply that it was dry and sunny, covered the whole country (even Scotland), but brought consistently high bases, often well over 7000’. There was the breaking of many PB’s … and of course a string of exceptionally long flights that tested UK coastlines to near their limit.

That is the backdrop, others will no doubt cover their flying experiences. My intention here is just to focus on the competition elements and primarily the rise of the DHPC as a dominant force within the UK club scene. Since the xcleague started I’ve follow the rise and fall of many clubs.  With no exception (I can find) club dominance relied on a group of talented, dedicated pilots coming together and gelling into a formidable team.  Whilst the club league is drawn from the top four club pilots, it also relies on strength in depth. Strong pilots ready to push from below, support and step in when necessary.  The Dales club has reached that enviable position.

The line graph shows the DHPC Club league position over the past 10 years. For some time it sat in the doldrums of mid table. During that time there were some excellent pilots who positioned near the top of the individual XCL, but not until the past five years did a settled group of very good pilots coalesce into a team.  The key word being team, whereby all aspects of a flight are unselfishly shared – from the planning, flying,  communications, logistics and generally helping each other to a common goal. Whenever any club has done well it’s always been based around a team effort. It’s also important to factor in the rotation of pilots where a club has a strong second string, and the Dales certainly has that in abundance. Also, at times, the influence of an individual who can act as a focal point for gathering, motivating and steering a group. It also pays to be prepared to travel … sometimes long distances at short notice.

Currently the DHPC sits 2nd, vying with the DSC (Derbyshire), but at 3660 to 3694 … it’s very close. 3rd placed Avon is almost 600 points behind.  With many big flights entered over the last great spell it gets that bit harder to close the gap. I’m not sure how many look at the various filters of the XCL but the club league is one to take an interest in.  I’ve deliberately mentioned no names … I mean, where do you start? The Club league is afterall … a team effort.

A few other things worth a mention from the competition scene.

BPCup.  This took place over four days at the end of April, based at the Dalesbridge centre , near Settle. Reports will appear elsewhere (May Skywords)  including a due Skywings. We couldn’t complain.  Two excellent tasks … the first a stunning flight from the Dales into the Lakes on a cracking day with many making goal. The following day a more testing flight north, with about six in goal. The third day was very taskable, but the lack of a decent southerly capable of handling the numbers (50) meant it was given over to free flying to spread the load. Each day the sun shone; only increasing wind cancelled out the final day. Overall, a very successful competition.

Northern Challenge Series (NCS).  Entries are steadily coming in and a number of tasks (9) have been flown to goal. The Brigsteer task got blitzed over four  days with four making goal.  Current leader, by a healthy margin, is Elliott Brown. Elliott has made goal on the Nappa and Longridge tasks, but has used up four of his eight scoring tasks so the chasing pack could soon catch him. If you haven’t tried any of the tasks (assuming you’re P rated) you may be surprised how challenging and addictive they can be … aside from the skills you can take from them. https://www.xcmap.net/

X Dales ‘25. Rob Ullersmann (quiet, shy, understudy) and I have been putting the final touches together for 2025. We’ve used a slightly different, pic n mix approach this year. We do like to explore the possibilities of hike & fly.  As previous years this is an element of the Dales Fly in.

Details of the full event and X Dales entry can be found via the Dales website. Or .. if the X Dales takes your fancy use this link.

https://www.xcflight.com/x-dales-25-challenge/

Finally, I’ve updated the site records. I think they're correct, but if you have sneaked in super flight l don’t know about, then let me know with the evidence.

Task days: I’ve not had any response to the idea so it's held for now. I still think it has merit, but  ..we'll see.

There have been more PB’s (personal bests) than I can keep track of. I’ll try and put a list together for the next copy of Skywords. The best/easiest and only way is if you let me know.  Last, but not least.  James Maxwell – could you tell me how you keep doing these big flights within one week of your Pilot rating? It took me decades.  

Below: Pendle NCS task. The excitement of tagging turnpoints. PEN 3 next
